channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: With New Seizures, Value of the Met’s Looted Artifacts Tops $95 Million
item.description: The Manhattan district attorney’s office said it secured the return of dozens of ancient artifacts in June, after a process that the museum described as collaborative.
item.dc:creator: Graham Bowley and Tom Mashberg

item.title: Jennifer Nettles on Her New Musical, ‘Giulia: The Poison Queen of Palermo’
item.description: The Sugarland singer Jennifer Nettles has written and is starring in a so-called “pop-eretta” titled “Giulia: The Poison Queen of Palermo.”
item.dc:creator: Alan Light and Sabrina Santiago
item.category: Perelman, Ronald O, Performing Arts Center
Giulia: The Poison Queen of Palermo (Play)
item.media:description: “It’s going to be entertaining,” Nettles said of the show, “and it has been supremely life-giving and exciting for me.”

item.title: Horror Fans Bid a Bittersweet Farewell to an Empty Mall Full of Zombies
item.description: Thousands flocked to a soon-to-close shopping center near Pittsburgh for a celebration of George A. Romero’s “Dawn of the Dead.”
item.dc:creator: Brian Raftery and Ross Mantle
item.media:credit: Ross Mantle for The New York Times

item.title: At 250, Has America Delivered on Its Classical Music Promise?
item.description: The classical music tradition in the United States is borrowed from Europe. But few could have anticipated how European it would remain.
item.dc:creator: Joseph Horowitz

item.title: When Sly Stone Got Married at M.S.G., He Had 20,000 Fans There With Him
item.description: A celebrity wedding at the New York arena? The funk and soul star did just that in 1974.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:description: Sly Stone and Kathy Silva at their wedding in 1974.

item.title: Even Absent, Trump Takes the Spotlight at a Night Celebrating Bill Maher
item.description: What the politician and the comic have in common became clear at the Mark Twain Prize ceremony, where the winner was called “underrated.”
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)
item.media:description: Bill Maher arriving at the ceremony honoring him on Sunday.

item.title: ‘The Bear’ Finale: I Hope You Saved Room for Cake
item.description: After a run of dazzling and frustrating ambition, the series closes with a pared-down menu and a touch of sweetness.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik
item.media:description: Ayo Edebiri and Jeremy Allen White in the final season of “The Bear,” now on Hulu.

item.title: The Best Movies of 2026 So Far, and Where to Watch Them
item.description: Now is a good time to catch up on films you may have missed. Our critics have thoughts.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Goodman
item.media:credit: Niko Tavernise/Universal Pictures and Amblin Entertainment
item.media:description: Josh O’Connor in a scene from “Disclosure Day.”

item.title: Little Island Significantly Scales Back Its Summer Schedule
item.description: The artistic director said Barry Diller, the former Hollywood magnate who provides the bulk of the park’s funding, “wants to take programming in a different direction.”
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez
item.media:credit: Bess Adler for The New York Times
item.media:description: A rehearsal of “The Counterfeit Opera” on Little Island last year.

item.title: ‘Elle’ and 10 More Shows to Watch on TV this Week
item.description: This “Legally Blonde” prequel premieres and networks air coverage around the nation’s 250th Independence Day.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:description: Lexi Minetree as Elle Woods in “Elle.”

item.title: Trump Wants to Own the Nation’s 250th Birthday. States Have Their Own Plans.
item.description: As partisan tensions swirl around festivities in Washington, red and blue states alike are leaning into complex history — and their own stories.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler

item.title: Washington Theater Leader Is Out on Opening Night of TLC Musical
item.description: The departing artistic director, Hana Sharif, made it clear in a resignation email that she was leaving Arena Stage under pressure.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Gabriella Demczuk for The New York Times
item.media:description: Under Hana Sharif’s leadership, the nonprofit Arena Stage presented several musicals with commercial ambitions, including “Swept Away,” a revised “Damn Yankees” and “Chez Joey,” a reimagined take on “Pal Joey.”

item.title: John Loring, Longtime Design Director at Tiffany & Company, Dies at 86
item.description: One of the country’s influential tastemakers, he oversaw the rapid expansion of Tiffany in the midst of seismic shifts in luxury retail.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Steve Campbell/Houston Chronicle, via Getty Images
item.media:description: John Loring in 2008. In 1979, he became the design director at Tiffany, a position he held for the next 30 years.

item.title: 7 Songs Recorded Live at Madison Square Garden
item.description: Hear tracks from Johnny Cash, Barbra Streisand and Billy Joel.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: Ron Galella/Ron Galella Collection, via Getty Images
item.media:description: June Carter Cash, left, and Johnny Cash at Madison Square Garden.

item.title: It’s Soccer, but Set to Disco. And May the Best Dancers Win.
item.description: “Discofoot,” a dance showdown and soccer game played to disco hits, originated in France. Last weekend, in honor of the World Cup, it had its U.S. premiere.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman and Gabriela Bhaskar

item.title: Three Great Documentaries to Stream
item.description: This month’s picks check the pulse of 1960s Paris, explore David Hockney’s artistry and paint an intimate portrait of mortality.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg
item.media:description: David Hockney in the documentary “A Bigger Splash.”

item.title: ‘Birthright’ Stages a Thrillingly Complex Conversation on Conversation Itself
item.description: Jonathan Spector’s ambitious drama about six Jewish friends and their shifting relationship with Israel stretches over three hours and nearly two decades.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: In “Birthright,” six American Jews, played by, from left, Zoë Winters, Molly Bernard, Molly Ranson, Nate Mann, Hale Appleman and Eli Gelb, connect while on an organized trip to Israel.

item.title: Blake Lively Seeks $8 Million in Legal Bills in Dispute With Justin Baldoni
item.description: After the actress settled her retaliation claims, a judge granted her fees for one part of the sprawling Hollywood case.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:description: Blake Lively is seeking more than $8 million in legal fees as part of her continuing legal dispute with Justin Baldoni.

item.title: ‘Bang My Box: The Robin Byrd Story’ Review: Sex for the City
item.description: A New York City pioneer of cable-access programming is given a lively tribute in this cheekily diverting documentary.
item.dc:creator: Jeannette Catsoulis
item.category: Bang My Box: The Robin Byrd Story (Movie)

item.title: How Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce Could Reveal Wedding Details
item.description: The playbook for celebrities’ wedding reveals has become a careful choreography — especially when you’re one of the biggest pop stars in the world.
item.dc:creator: Madison Malone Kircher
item.media:description: Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce in Brooklyn last month. Since the couple announced their engagement last year, they have only hinted at a few details of their nuptials.

item.title: John Oliver Dives Into Trump’s Pet Project: A ‘Giant Duck Death Trap’
item.description: The “Last Week Tonight” host said the United States was “dealing with a literal swamp of corruption” for its 250th birthday.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Last Week Tonight With John Oliver (TV Program)
Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)

item.title: Daveigh Chase, ‘Lilo & Stitch’ Voice Actor, Died of AIDS, Officials Say
item.description: Ms. Chase was the voice of Lilo and also played the child villain in the horror film “The Ring.” She died in Los Angeles this month.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:description: The actress Daveigh Chase in 2011. Her father said she was homeless before she died this month in Los Angeles.

item.title: Review: The Choreographer Lucinda Childs’s Persistence of Vision
item.description: “Momentary Reprise,” a program at Bard SummerScape, spanned Childs’s career, showing how she settled on a style in the 1970s and stuck with it.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert
item.category: Fisher, Richard B, Center for the Performing Arts at Bard College

item.title: On the ‘Lux Tour’, Rosalía Shape Shifts Through Dance
item.description: On the “Lux” tour, the pop star mingles dance genres, starting the show on pointe. As with her dip into opera, her technique might not be perfect, but the intention is good.
item.dc:creator: Margaret Fuhrer

item.title: 22 New Books to Read in July: Colson Whitehead, Alice Oseman, Daniel Mason and More
item.description: Novels by Colson Whitehead and Daniel Mason, the final “Heartstopper” graphic novel, gripping thrillers, lush romantasy, true crime and more.

item.title: ‘House of the Dragon’ Season 3 Episode 2 Recap: Throne of Blood
item.description: Rhaenyra is plunged back into grief. What will that mean for the fate of the realm?
item.dc:creator: Sean T. Collins
item.media:description: Rhaenyra (Emma D’Arcy) suffered another major loss. Will it be in vain?

item.title: ‘Supergirl’ Falters at the Box Office, Testing DC Studios’ Reboot
item.description: Overall, ticket sales for the weekend were up 21 percent from last year. But the expensive “Supergirl” debuted as a disappointing second, with an estimated $38 million.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: ‘The Bear’ Season 5 Recap: What Worked? What Lingers?
item.description: The final season was in many ways a recapitulation of the whole series. Here’s a look at where Carmy, Syd and the rest of the gang wound up.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.media:description: Sydney (Ayo Edebiri) and Carmy (Jeremy Allen White) had a suddenly different dynamic in Season 5 of “The Bear.”

item.title: Bill Maher Receives Mark Twain Prize at the Kennedy Center
item.description: The late-night host leaned into his spat with President Trump while receiving a top comedy award at the institution that once bore the president’s name.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)
item.media:description: Bill Maher at the Kennedy Center on Sunday. He has recently likened President Trump to an emperor, mocked his approval ratings, and said the president had done “things that are racist, misogynistic, anti-democratic and corrupt.”

item.title: The Artist Uman’s Technicolor Paintings of Rural Life
item.description: Ahead of a survey exhibition, Uman discusses skipping art-world functions, living on a farm and making work on the floor.
item.dc:creator: Lauren O’Neill-Butler

item.title: Tony Brown, Host of Public Affairs Show Aimed at Black Audiences, Dies at 93
item.description: On “Tony Brown’s Journal,” he interviewed guests like Jesse Jackson and Lena Horne, and made programming decisions by asking himself, “Will it help Black people?”
item.dc:creator: Joseph Berger

item.title: Phoebe Bridgers, Sombr: Songs to Know This Week
item.description: Phoebe Bridgers re-emerges, Sombr gets shy and PJ Harvey heads for the cosmos.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles

item.title: 5 of My Favorite Onscreen Moments of 2026, So Far
item.description: These are the images that have stuck with me, from the uplifting to the grotesque — and with a surprising amount of dancing.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips

item.title: ‘Sinatra, The Musical’: Regrets, We Have a Few
item.description: A West End production has the hits, and Joel Harper-Jackson brings swagger and style to the leading role. But where’s the pizazz?
item.dc:creator: Matt Wolf
item.media:description: Joel Harper-Jackson as Frank Sinatra in “Sinatra The Musical.”

item.title: Bruno Bischofberger Dies at 86; Gallerist Championed Warhol and Basquiat
item.description: He was a quintessential art dealer of the go-go 1980s, when the booming market turned painters into celebrities.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: 6 Travel Podcasts to Inspire Your Next Trip
item.description: These shows offer a mix of evocative travelogues, practical tips, and expert recommendations for destinations across the globe.
item.dc:creator: Emma Dibdin

item.title: Maren Hassinger Likes Her Art With a Twist
item.description: Her lively, gnarly sculpture stars in her biggest exhibition yet, “Living Moving Growing,” at the Berkeley Art Museum and Pacific Film Archive.
item.dc:creator: Jori Finkel and Aubrey Trinnaman
item.category: Berkeley Art Museum and Pacific Film Archive
item.media:description: Maren Hassinger with her newest sculpture: “Monument (Pyramid),” a 10-foot-tall pyramid made of interwoven redwood branches, set within a grove of mature oak trees.

item.title: Jodie Turner-Smith Likes Her Passports Tattered and Torn
item.description: “Every stamp reminds me the world is enormous and generous, and that I owe it to my daughter to keep showing her how big it is,” the actress said.
item.dc:creator: Kathryn Shattuck

item.title: Come for the Books, Stay for the Power Tools
item.description: Libraries across the country are lending American Girl dolls, original artwork, musical instruments and ghost-hunting kits to help patrons save money and share resources.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Diamond and Gabrielle Lamontagne

item.title: How Teaching A.I. Endangered Languages Can Help Save Them
item.description: By feeding centuries-old nursery rhymes and folklore recordings into their own model, linguists in Louisiana hope to help a community control its digital destiny.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams

item.title: A floating convenience store appears in Toronto
item.description: “Global Convenience,” an art installation honoring the city’s role as a World Cup host, is a near-perfect replica of a corner shop. Someone got stranded after swimming out to it last week.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.media:credit: Trevor Wheatley, Cosmo Dean, and Puncture Design

item.title: Man Pleads Guilty to Fraud in Sale of Art to Springsteen’s Manager
item.description: Thomas Doyle was accused of unlawfully pocketing most of the proceeds from the sale of a painting by Gustave Courbet to Bruce Springsteen’s manager.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:description: Gustave Courbet’s “Mother and Child on a Hammock,” which is dated to roughly 1848.

item.title: Ping-Pong’s Endless Summer at Rockaway Beach
item.description: Six artist-designed tables commissioned by Public Art Fund serve up serious fun in “Between Tides” at Beach 67. (Just take the A train.)
item.dc:creator: Hilarie M. Sheets and Jonah Rosenberg
item.media:credit: Jonah Rosenberg for The New York Times

item.title: Ann Blyth, Oscar-Nominated ‘Mildred Pierce’ Actress, Dies at 98
item.description: She was just 17 when she played Joan Crawford’s ungrateful daughter in the classic 1945 melodrama. It was the high point of a robust but short film career.
item.dc:creator: Anita Gates and Zachary Woolfe

item.title: American Ballet Theater’s ‘Don Quixote’: You May Laugh. And Nobody Dies.
item.description: American Ballet Theater’s artistic director, Susan Jaffe, teams up with the régisseur Susan Jones for a new staging of the comic romp.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas and Vincent Tullo
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jaffe, right, with dancers. “Things are stale when they are being performed by rote,” she said.

item.title: The Man Who Cried Goooooooooooal
item.description: The Telemundo announcer Andrés Cantor must train to deliver his famed scoring celebration for the World Cup, where he could call 20 games in about a month.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan and Steven Molina Contreras
item.media:credit: Steven Molina Contreras for The New York Times

item.title: Five International Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s titles include Pride Month picks from Brazil, Sweden and Croatia; a soccer drama from Ireland; and a comedy from Italy.
item.dc:creator: Devika Girish
item.category: The Last One for the Road (Movie)

item.title: ‘Pride’: A Tale Retold, Zhuzhed Up With Extra Camp
item.description: A new musical based on the 2014 movie “Pride” trades restrained sentimentality for all-in emotion and flamboyance.
item.dc:creator: Houman Barekat
item.media:description: “Pride” at the National Theater in London. The story’s bittersweet melodrama and tender evocation of solidarity lend themselves well to a musical theater treatment.

item.title: Larry David’s Comedy on HBO and America’s Unhappy 250th Birthday
item.description: On the one hand, the 250th anniversary has given us presidential blood sport. On the other comes a show about star-spangled nothing.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik
item.category: Life, Larry and the Pursuit of Unhappiness (TV Program)
item.media:description: In “Life, Larry and the Pursuit of Unhappiness,” Larry David inserts himself awkwardly into watershed moments from American history.

item.title: For the U.S.’s 250th Birthday, a German Declaration of Independence Goes on Show
item.description: The presentation of an original German-language translation in Berlin is an opportunity to consider the shared history of the United States and Germany.
item.dc:creator: Thomas Rogers

item.title: With ‘Supergirl,’ Milly Alcock is Learning to Accept the Fear
item.description: After “House of the Dragon,” a starring role in another big franchise, “Supergirl,” gave Milly Alcock pause. It’s “this new gift of learning to accept the fear.”
item.dc:creator: Clarissa Cruz
item.media:credit: Molly Matalon for The New York Times

item.title: 11 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: ‘Jackass: Best and Last’: A Moving Look at Male Friendship and Aging (Really)
item.description: While it may seem as if these pranksters have cheated death, the latest movie in the series starring Johnny Knoxville reminds us they’re mere mortals.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: In ‘The Vampire Lestat,’ He’s Partying Like a Bloodsucking Rock Star
item.description: Sam Reid has gone glam in the latest season of this horror series, originally titled “Anne Rice’s Interview With the Vampire.”
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg and David Billet
item.category: Anne Rice's Interview With the Vampire (TV Program)
item.media:credit: David Billet for The New York Times

item.title: 39 Fun Things to Do With Your Kids in N.Y.C. This Summer
item.description: Harvest the fruits of the local ice cream scene, celebrate the World Cup and the nation’s birthday, and enjoy a variety of diversions outdoors.
item.dc:creator: Oliver Strand
item.category: Il Laboratorio del Gelato (Manhattan, NY, Restaurant)
Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts
The SpongeBob Movie: Search for SquarePants (Movie)

item.title: Gracie Abrams on ‘Nepo’ Label, Writing With Paul Mescal and Her New Album
item.description: On her new album, “Daughter From Hell,” the 26-year-old star confronts growing up in a famous family and lessons from songwriting.
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli, Jon Caramanica and Caroline Tompkins
item.media:credit: Caroline Tompkins for The New York Times

item.title: Mewgenics Is a Mischievous Game About Breeding Cats
item.description: The year’s most interesting games also include a bodycam shooter in a Hong Kong underworld and a cryptographic puzzle drama.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: ‘Strung’ Review: A Tease of a Thriller
item.description: Chloe Bailey, Lynn Whitfield and Lucien Laviscount have delivered more of a camp classic than a taut psychological film.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: Late Night Grades the Attractions at the Great American State Fair
item.description: The fair has everything, Jimmy Fallon said, including “a Trump approval rating roller coaster, which has the biggest drop in history.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix

item.title: Alannah Keyser Is Kicked Off ‘Love Island USA’ for Racial Slur
item.description: After Alannah Keyser made her debut on Sunday, viewers quickly found a social media video in which she uses a slur while singing.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:description: Alannah Keyser joined “Love Island USA” on Sunday.
